title: Hims vs. Ro vs. Found vs. Noom: We Tested 4 Weight Loss Platforms and Found a Clear Winner
summary: We enrolled in all four platforms for 90 days, tracked prescriptions, coaching quality, app experience, and outcomes. One platform stood apart — and one was a waste of money.

pros: Board-certified prescribers with real follow-up
Transparent, all-in pricing — no surprise fees
Metabolic health coaching included
Broad formulary: semaglutide, tirzepatide, orlistat
cons: Higher monthly cost than Ro or Hims
App UX is functional but not polished
Longer intake process than competitors

body:
Weight loss platforms have exploded in the last three years, largely on the back of GLP-1 drugs like semaglutide and tirzepatide. We spent 90 days testing Hims, Ro, Found, and Noom to find out which is actually worth your money.

## What We Tested

We evaluated each platform on five criteria: ease of sign-up and intake, prescriber quality, medication availability, app and coaching experience, and value for money.

## The Results

Found emerged as the clear winner, offering the most comprehensive metabolic health approach, strong prescriber follow-up, and transparent pricing. Ro was a strong second. Noom's coaching model felt dated. Hims had the slickest UX but the thinnest clinical layer.

## Bottom Line

If you're looking for GLP-1 support with real clinical infrastructure, Found is your best bet in 2026. Budget-conscious? Ro's generic semaglutide program is a close second.

title: Hims vs. Roman vs. Keeps: Which ED and Hair Loss Platform Actually Delivers?
summary: Three of the biggest men's health DTC brands, head to head. We compared prescriptions, pricing, shipping, and follow-up care across ED and hair loss treatments.

pros: Roman offers the clearest ED pricing with no hidden fees
Keeps has the strongest hair loss protocol of any DTC brand
Fast prescription turnaround on both platforms
Genuine physician oversight, not PA-only
cons: No single platform excels at both categories
Hims upsells aggressively throughout the funnel
Keeps has minimal offerings outside hair loss

body:
The men's DTC telehealth market is dominated by three brands: Hims, Roman, and Keeps. We put all three through their paces across two of their most popular treatment categories: ED medication and hair loss prevention.

## ED Treatment

Roman leads on clinical follow-up and transparent pricing. Hims has the best app experience but upsells aggressively. Keeps focuses primarily on hair loss and is thinner on ED offerings.

## Hair Loss Treatment

Keeps is the specialist here and it shows — better hair loss protocol, cleaner finasteride pricing, and stronger dermatologist involvement. Hims is flashier; Roman is more clinical.

## Bottom Line

For ED, go with Roman. For hair loss, go with Keeps. Hims is a reasonable all-in-one if you want everything in one app and don't mind the marketing noise.

title: Cerebral vs. Done vs. Talkiatry: We Reviewed 3 Psychiatry Platforms and Found One Clear Leader
summary: We evaluated all three psychiatry platforms on provider quality, wait times, medication management, and what happens when things go wrong.

scorecard.bestFor: Ongoing psychiatric care with board-certified MDs
pros: Board-certified psychiatrists only — no PA-led care
Strong continuity; same provider across appointments
Insurance-integrated billing reduces out-of-pocket cost
Handles complex diagnoses beyond ADHD and anxiety
cons: Slower to onboard than Cerebral or Done
Not available in all states
No therapy offering — psychiatry only

body:
Access to psychiatric care in the US remains genuinely hard. We spent three months on Cerebral, Done, and Talkiatry to understand which platform delivers real clinical value.

## Provider Quality

Talkiatry uses only board-certified psychiatrists and it shows in appointment depth and continuity. Cerebral's model mixes NPs and MDs with inconsistent results. Done is ADHD-focused and good at that narrow use case.

## Medication Management

Talkiatry and Cerebral both handle prescriptions in-app. Done's pharmacy partnerships are smooth for stimulant prescriptions but limited outside ADHD.

## Bottom Line

Talkiatry is the clear leader for general psychiatric care. Done is the right call if ADHD is your primary concern. Cerebral has improved since its 2022 controversies but still trails on consistency.

title: Midi vs. Alloy vs. Evernow: Menopause and Hormone Care Platforms, Ranked
summary: We tested all three menopause telehealth platforms over 12 weeks, evaluating intake, provider expertise, HRT options, and ongoing care quality.

scorecard.bestFor: Comprehensive menopause care with specialist providers
pros: Menopause-certified clinicians on every case
Broadest HRT formulary: patches, gels, pills, testosterone
Thorough intake covers symptoms, labs, and history
Strong follow-up cadence — proactive check-ins
cons: Longer time-to-first-prescription than Alloy
Higher subscription cost
Insurance coverage is limited

body:
Menopause care has been underserved for decades. Three DTC platforms — Midi, Alloy, and Evernow — are trying to change that. We spent 12 weeks on each.

## Intake and Provider Quality

Midi's intake is the most thorough, and its providers are menopause-specialist certified. Alloy is faster to prescribe. Evernow has strong content but thinner clinical depth.

## HRT Options

All three offer estradiol patches, gels, and pills. Midi and Alloy also offer progesterone and testosterone. Evernow's formulary is narrower.

## Bottom Line

Midi wins on clinical quality. Alloy wins on speed and simplicity. Evernow is worth it for education, but we'd want more from the clinical layer.

title: Curology vs. Apostrophe vs. Agency: Best Acne and Anti-Aging Rx?
summary: Custom Rx skincare vs. full dermatology care vs. boutique formulations — we tested all three for three months and tracked real skin changes.

pros: Board-certified dermatologists, not PAs
Widest treatment range: tretinoin, azelaic acid, clindamycin, and more
Photo-based async model works well in practice
Transparent ingredient disclosure on all formulations
cons: Pricier than Curology for comparable acne treatment
No live video visits — async only
Slower initial response time vs. Agency

body:
Prescription skincare DTC has matured. We spent 12 weeks on Curology, Apostrophe, and Agency to find out which platform delivers the best skin results.

## Custom Formulations

Curology and Agency both ship custom-compounded topicals. Apostrophe leans on standard Rx products from real dermatologists. Agency's formulations were the most aggressive and showed the fastest results for acne.

## Provider Access

Apostrophe wins here — actual board-certified dermatologists reviewing your photos, not PAs alone. Curology is PA-heavy. Agency is async-only.

## Bottom Line

For acne, Agency's formula speed is compelling. For anti-aging and broader skin concerns, Apostrophe's provider quality is worth the premium. Curology remains the best value for mild to moderate acne.

title: Teladoc vs. MDLive vs. Amazon Clinic: Which Is Worth Paying For?
summary: We compared the three biggest general telehealth platforms on wait times, provider quality, prescription speed, and total cost of care.

scorecard.bestFor: Insured patients needing consistent on-demand care
pros: Broadest insurance network of any telehealth platform
Consistent quality at scale across all 50 states
Live video and messaging options
Handles chronic condition management, not just acute care
cons: UI is dated and clunky compared to competitors
Wait times can spike during peak hours
Amazon Clinic is faster and cheaper for simple conditions

body:
For acute care and primary care needs, Teladoc, MDLive, and Amazon Clinic dominate the on-demand telehealth market. We put all three to the test with real clinical visits.

## Wait Times

Amazon Clinic is asynchronous for most conditions — submit your info, get a Rx in hours. Teladoc and MDLive both offer live video within 15 minutes for most slots.

## Provider Quality

All three use licensed physicians and NPs. Teladoc's quality is most consistent at scale. MDLive's providers felt most engaged in visits. Amazon Clinic trades depth for speed.

## Bottom Line

For fast UTI or strep treatment, Amazon Clinic wins on simplicity and price. For anything more complex, Teladoc's infrastructure and coverage integrations make it the right long-term choice.

title: Bummed vs. HemAway vs. GI Alliance: Anorectal Care, Compared
summary: Anorectal conditions affect millions but few talk about them. We reviewed three platforms offering remote and in-person care for hemorrhoids, fissures, and related conditions.

scorecard.bestFor: First-line remote care for hemorrhoids and anal fissures
pros: Only DTC platform built exclusively for anorectal care
Colorectal-specialist prescribers — not generalists
Rx compounded topicals shipped within days
Genuinely thorough intake — better than most GP visits
cons: Procedural care (banding, sclerotherapy) requires in-person referral
Limited brand awareness means less community/peer data
Not covered by insurance

body:
Hemorrhoids, anal fissures, and related anorectal conditions affect an estimated 75% of people at some point in their lives — yet the category is dramatically underserved in DTC telehealth. We reviewed three platforms addressing this gap.

## Bummed

Bummed is the only fully DTC telehealth platform purpose-built for anorectal care. It offers async consultation, Rx compounded topicals, and dietary guidance. The intake is thorough, the prescribers are colorectal-adjacent specialists, and the follow-up care is genuinely better than what most patients get from a GP.

## HemAway

HemAway focuses on OTC products with a light clinical layer. It's less a telehealth platform and more a consumer brand with a quiz. Not a like-for-like comparison.

## GI Alliance

GI Alliance is a national GI practice network — in-person, not DTC. It's the right choice when you need procedural care. For early-stage or mild conditions, it's overkill.

## Bottom Line

For remote, first-line anorectal care, Bummed is the standout. It fills a real gap and delivers clinical quality that surprises for the price point.
